title: One Small Reach This Week
subtitle: A short activity for when the distance between you and your partner has grown without you noticing
excerpt: A practical activity for the relationship that has quietly grown more parallel than connected. Asks for one small gesture of reach: a touch, a question, a coffee together. Not a big conversation. A small physical gesture toward each other.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education, drawing on the research of John Gottman on the role of small positive interactions in maintaining relationship connection.

content.text: A simple activity for the relationship drift that happens without drama. Not estrangement. Not conflict. Just two people who have been busy and are now more alongside each other than with each other. One small reach is enough to change the direction.
This week, do one small thing that reaches toward your partner. The reach is physical or conversational, not logistical. Logistics keep a household running. A reach reconnects two people.
A touch on the shoulder as you walk past. Not a task handoff. Just a touch.
A question you actually want to know the answer to. Not 'did you sort the insurance?'
A coffee together with nothing else open on a screen.
Bringing them a cup without being asked.

Relationships research shows that small positive interactions accumulate. One reach is not a fix. But a week of one small reach a day is a direction change. The couple who does this consistently does not need the big repair conversations as often, because the account has credit in it.
Do the one small thing. Notice the response. Do it again next week.

Written for homemade.education. Based on John Gottman's work on small bids for connection in long relationships. His best-known finding: five small positive moments for every negative one keeps a couple steady. One small daily reach is one of those moments.
